name: David Morales
occupation: Regional Sales Manager at a pharmaceutical company
backstory: David was promoted to regional manager eighteen months ago. The role comes with a new peer group — people who drive luxury cars, wear designer watches, and vacation in places David's never been. He grew up middle-class in Tucson and still carries some of that identity, but he's spending to match what he perceives as the standard for someone in his position. His wife has started noticing the credit card bills. He coaches his daughter's soccer team on Saturdays, which is the most grounded he feels all week. He's good at setting goals when he has a clear target, but right now the target feels like 'keep up.'
emotional_triggers: Coworker conversations about purchases, vacations, and lifestyle
LinkedIn culture — success performance, 'level up' messaging
'You deserve it' and 'invest in yourself' marketing language
Feeling like an imposter in his new role
existing_strengths: Strong community ties through soccer coaching
Genuinely good at goal-setting and follow-through in professional contexts
Grew up resourceful — knows how to be happy with less, but has lost touch with it
Respected by his team at work for being authentic
current_situation: David is looking at a $4,200 watch online. His current watch works fine — he bought it five years ago and likes it. But three colleagues at a dinner last week all had luxury watches and he felt out of place. He's rationalizing it as a 'reward for the promotion' even though the promotion was 18 months ago. He's not sure why he's hesitating.
what_success_looks_like: David recognizes the social proof pattern, reconnects with what actually makes him feel confident (coaching, his team's respect), and decides on his own terms — not based on what three guys at dinner were wearing.
